attributes.stem:
A 70-year-old woman is brought to her general practitioner by her son who describes concerns that ‘his mother is not like her usual self’. He describes increasing issues with memory, where the patient has needed more assistance with household tasks. She has missed several scheduled appointments and tends to repeat herself in conversation. The patient’s son also describes a recent change in mood, feeling his mother is less motivated than usual. Several months ago, the patient went to the emergency department after a fall, where a CT brain at that time revealed no acute intracranial pathology.

Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, hypertension, and vitamin B12 deficiency. She takes thyroxine, perindopril and receives vitamin B12 injections. She has never smoked, and she denies the use of alcohol or illicit drugs. No obvious abnormalities were noted on physical examination. A mini-mental state examination reveals deficiencies in orientation (time), recall, and language, with a total score of 19/30. A urinalysis conducted today came back negative.
attributes.stemQuestion: Considering the likely diagnosis, which one of the following is the condition’s underlying pathology? Choose the single best answer.
options: Cytoplasmic spherical inclusions composed of tau fibrils
Dopaminergic neuron degeneration of the substantia nigra
attributes.explanation:
Dementia (termed major neurocognitive disorder in DSM-V) is defined by significant cognitive decline involving at least 1 of the domains (learning and memory, language, executive function, complex attention, visuospatial, social cognition) that interferes with independence and daily living. There are several neurodegenerative conditions causing dementia, of which they share a number of clinical symptoms. Neuroimaging commonly shows cerebral atrophy, but certain patterns may suggest specific neurodegenerative disorders, as discussed below.

The most common cause of dementia is Alzheimer's disease (AD). Anhedonia, apathy and short-term memory loss are particularly associated with Alzheimer's disease, as in this case. Beta-amyloid plaques, neurofibrillary tangles and neuritic plaques are essential pathologic changes of AD. Hence this is the correct answer. The characteristic finding on neuroimaging is medial temporal lobe atrophy. Important neurodegenerative disorders to consider as differentials are discussed below, these options are incorrect as the clinical features are not consistent with the case.

Lewy bodies consist of alpha-synuclein aggregates mainly. These are seen in Dementia with Lewy Bodies and other disorders including Parkinson's disease, and Alzheimer's disease. Clinical features of the condition seen in the patient include postural instability (via the recent fall), apathy and avolition. However, core clinical features of visual hallucinations, REM sleep behaviour disorder, and Parkinsonism are not described in the stem.  

Cytoplasmic spherical inclusions composed of tau fibrils, otherwise known as Pick bodies, are a feature of frontotemporal dementia (FTD). This pathological feature is not necessary to make a diagnosis of FTD; however, assists in making a pathological diagnosis. Patients with early FTD will present with disinhibition, apathy, and compulsive behaviours. In contrast to Alzheimer’s disease, FTD presents with loss of social cognition as an early feature of disease.

Dopaminergic neuron degeneration of the substantia nigra is seen in Parkinson's disease. The cardinal features of the disease are bradykinesia, resting tremor and rigidity. There are no specific neuroimaging features, but it is sometimes used to exclude other causes (e.g., tumour), particularly if clinical features are atypical.

Vascular dementia is the second most common cause of dementia, after Alzheimer’s disease. It is seen in patients with hypertension (as this patient has), and typically presents with stepwise cognitive decline, but may have a more insidious onset, particularly if infarcts occur in small vessels.  Pseudobulbar affect and neuropsychiatric signs such as depression may also be present. Neuroimaging may show white-matter hyperintensity, infarct, and cerebral atrophy, with diffuse small vessel atherosclerosis.
learningPoints: Dementia is characterised by a decline in at least 1 of the cognitive domain that is severe enough to interfere with independence in daily activities.
Alzheimer disease is the most common cause of major neurocognitive disorder and  anhedonia, apathy and short-term memory loss are particularly associated.
Beta-amyloid plaques, neurofibrillary tangles and neuritic plaques are essential pathologic changes of Alzheimer disease.
references: Larson EB. Evaluation of cognitive impairment and dementia. In: UpToDate, DeKosky ST, Schmader KE, Mendez MF, Tung GA (Ed), Wolters Kluwer. Updated November 2, 2022. Accessed April 2, 2024. https://www.uptodate.com/contents/the-red-eye-evaluation-and-management
Arvanitakis Z, Shah RC, Bennett DA. Diagnosis and management of dementia: review. JAMA. 2019 Oct 22;322(16):1589–99.
Dyer SM, Laver K, Pond CD, Cumming RG, Whitehead C, Crotty M. Clinical practice guidelines and principles of care for people with dementia in Australia. Aust Fam Physician. 2016 Dec;45(12):884–9.
